mechanism: Experimental manipulation of perceived social standing relative to reference population triggers stimulus-response pathway affecting appetite regulation via attentional/motivational reweighting of intrinsic hunger signals and energy acquisition valuation.

Framing of comparison temporal direction (past vs. future) affects salience of evidence and alignment with prior ideological commitments, modulating belief update and behavioral choice through top-down schema matching.

Attention-based multiattribute choice model where salient extrinsic cue shifts decision-making mindset from prosocial/intrinsic to cost-benefit/calculative calculus, reducing net motivation for target behavior via reduced weight on shrouded intrinsic attributes.

Face-based trait inference (competence, morality, sociability, attractiveness) automatically activates within milliseconds and biases selective attention to and evaluative judgment of scientific information via implicit person-perception modules.

Belief in free will modulates salience and causal attribution via intention-attribution mechanism: stronger free-will belief increases perceived locus of control in observed agents, shifting causal inference toward internal dispositions and away from situational constraints.

Evolutionary pressure from competitive multiagent learning dynamics: when flexible agents interact, costly-action role becomes vulnerable to learning suboptimal behavior; higher vulnerability selects for rigid innate preference bias (hedonic reward) to compensate for learning disadvantage.
